blurb: Unglamorous but a pollinator powerhouse — the region's #2 plant for specialist native bees: bright yellow daisies cupped in sticky, curled-back resinous bracts on a tough, drought-proof native of hot, lean, disturbed ground. A self-sowing pioneer; deadhead if you want to keep it in bounds.
water: Low; very xeric — a tough pioneer of dry, lean ground
spread: Clumping biennial/short-lived perennial that self-sows readily — a pioneer of disturbed ground; deadhead to limit spread
seasons: Resinous gray-green foliage; yellow daisies cupped in distinctive sticky, curled-back green bracts from midsummer into fall
wildlife: A keystone for specialist native bees (about 68 species — the region's #2 specialist-bee plant) plus many bees, butterflies and beetles; seeds for birds
deer: Resistant — the resinous, bitter foliage is left alone
toxic: Non-toxic to people (the sticky resin was a traditional medicinal); can accumulate selenium on seleniferous soils, a minor livestock note

care.hardiness: A native of the dry interior West — ranging from the Canadian prairies (Manitoba) south to Texas and across to Idaho and Arizona, and through Colorado from the plains to ~8,500 ft — so it is thoroughly cold-hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters.[1][2] It is a biennial or short-lived perennial that reproduces by seed.[1]
care.planting: Easiest sown direct onto lean, sunny, disturbed ground; nursery starts also transplant in spring after the mid-May last frost. It wants the open, unimproved soil it colonizes in the wild, so don't amend or pamper the site. In pots use a gritty, fast-draining mix in full sun, but this is really an open-ground pioneer rather than a container plant.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — a plant of open dry prairie, roadsides, and waste ground; the leaves even turn edge-on to the sun. It will not perform in shade.[2][3]
care.soil: Lean, dry, well-drained ground — sandy loam to loam, often poor or disturbed; it readily tolerates Boulder's alkaline clay and is most at home on the bare, depleted, or recently disturbed soils other plants avoid.[1][2]
care.water: Very low; genuinely xeric once established. A deep taproot (to ~6 ft) plus resinous foliage make it strongly drought-resistant — it needs essentially no supplemental water in our climate and is a tough pioneer of hot, dry ground.[1]
care.spacing: Allow about 12–18 in. between plants; each makes a bushy, branching clump 1–3 ft tall and wide.[2]
care.propagation: By seed only — it does not divide. The wind-dispersed seed (each with a pappus) germinates readily; surface-sow on bare ground in fall or early spring, as the fine seed needs light. After a brief cold period seed germinates in just a few days. There is no vegetative method; it perpetuates itself by self-sowing.[1]
care.sow: Surface-sow on bare, disturbed soil in fall or early spring — press the seed in but leave it uncovered, since it needs light. Fall sowing lets the natural cold break dormancy; germination is fast (a few days) once conditions warm.[1]
care.stratify: A short cold-moist period breaks dormancy and speeds germination — fall-sown seed chills naturally over winter, after which it germinates within a few days.[1]
care.bloom: Blooms midsummer into fall — roughly July through September/October — with yellow daisies cupped in the distinctive sticky, curled-back resinous green bracts, peaking in August when late-season forage is scarce.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Essentially no care once established. Its one demand on the gardener is restraint of its self-sowing: deadhead spent flower heads before the seed ripens to keep this enthusiastic pioneer in bounds, leaving a few if you want it to persist.[1]
care.selfsow: A prolific, wind-dispersed self-sower and a pioneer of disturbed ground — it readily colonizes bare soil and can spread, so deadhead before seed set to limit it (or let a few heads shatter to keep the patch going). Best sited where its ruderal, pollinator-habitat character is welcome rather than in a tidy formal bed.[1]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free — the resinous, bitter foliage is unbothered by most pests and is left alone by deer and livestock. On seleniferous soils it is a facultative selenium accumulator, a minor caveat where it might be grazed; in the home garden its main 'fault' is over-enthusiastic self-sowing.[1]
care.companions: Other xeric, sun-loving Front-Range natives of lean ground — blue grama, sulphur flower buckwheat (*Eriogonum umbellatum*), dotted gayfeather (*Liatris punctata*), rabbitbrush, and the prairie asters — in a pollinator or restoration planting where its specialist-bee value shines.[2]

edible.summary: Not a food — bitter and resinous, with only documented medicinal/folk use, not culinary; grow it as a native pollinator plant, not for eating.[1][2]
edible.caution: Curlycup gumweed is not eaten as food: the foliage and flower heads are coated in a sticky resin and are intensely bitter (tannins, volatile oils, resins, bitter alkaloids and glucosides), which is why livestock and deer avoid it. Its only documented human uses are medicinal/folk — the resin was traditionally used for respiratory complaints (asthma, bronchitis, cough) and skin irritations such as poison-ivy/poison-oak rash — not as a vegetable or seasoning, and that historical medicinal use is not a basis for casual consumption. It is also a facultative selenium accumulator on seleniferous soils, so plants from such ground can carry elevated selenium. Treat it as inedible.[1][2]
